Description: One of the earliest and most famous Gen 1 ROM hacks (by Koolboyman), set in the all-new Rijon region with 100+ Pokémon, new towns, and a full post-game. A complete reimagining rather than a simple edit.

A story-driven Silver hack by miksy91 set in the original world of Suden, playing more like a classic JRPG (à la Final Fantasy/Chrono Trigger) than a gym-badge journey, with an Elemental Orbs plot and eight optional sidequests.

Proper Name: Pokémon Green (Pocket Monsters: Green, Japan-only; English translation)
Description: The original 1996 Japan-only release that launched the franchise alongside Red; later updated and reissued as Blue. A turn-based RPG played in English via a fan translation.
A Gold-based hack by Freako set shortly after Gold/Silver, with new starters, a brand-new region, original story, and Fakemon.
A continuation/sequel to Pokémon Bronze, again built on Gold, expanding the region and roster.
A novelty hack of Red where every Pokémon is 'Zardified' into a Charizard-themed, Fire-type creature. Companion release to Pokémon Zard.
A brutally difficult 'kaizo' overhaul of Crystal with revamped trainer teams, higher levels, and competitive movesets aimed at hardcore players.
A Crystal hack that reimagines Johto with new Fakemon, retyped Pokémon, and a fresh take on the Gen 2 adventure.
A heavily customized Gold hack by Acegikmo with extensive tile, map, and sprite edits; the hero is a nose and the rival is a pig, leaning into crude, random humor.
A hack that rebuilds the leaked 1997 Spaceworld Gold/Silver demo into a complete, polished game using the demo's beta Pokémon and graphics.
A sprite hack of Super Mario Land turned into a Pokémon platformer starring Pikachu, with enemies replaced by Pokémon and Ash standing in for Princess Daisy.
A Red-based hack where you play as a Team Rocket grunt instead of the hero, experiencing Kanto from the villains' side.

Proper Name: Mega Man V DX (Rockman World 5 DX)
Description: A colorized, enhanced hack of the Game Boy game Mega Man V (Rockman World 5), a side-scrolling action-platformer.
A parody hack of Yellow themed around Jigglypuff and the color pink, with Jigglypuff following you in place of Pikachu.
Companion to Pokémon Chari; a Red hack where every Pokémon becomes a Charizard variant.
An official McDonald's promotional retro platformer (2023) starring Grimace on a skateboarding birthday adventure.
A Crystal hack with a festive holiday theme, set in a wintry Christmas-themed region.
A hack that completes and polishes the leaked 1997 Spaceworld Gold demo into a full game.
A Crystal hack by Pia Carrot inspired by the anime's Orange Islands, with an island-hopping journey.
A 'kaizo' difficulty hack of Pokémon Blue with much tougher trainers and battles.
A completed, playable version of the 1997 Spaceworld Gold/Silver demo, restoring its beta content into a full game.
A cancelled official Pokémon puzzle game for Game Boy Color, based on nonogram puzzles; survives only as a leaked prototype.

Proper Name: Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: Here Comes Team Great Rocket! (fan translation)
Description: The Japan-only sequel to the Pokémon Trading Card Game GBC title, a card-battling RPG; played in English via fan translation.
An acclaimed open-world Crystal hack that lets you tackle Johto and Kanto nonlinearly, choosing your own starter, route, and gym order.
A highly regarded Crystal hack set in the new Naljo region, adding a mining minigame, new types, and a deep original story.
A Crystal hack that retools Johto into a tighter, better-balanced and more challenging Gen 2 experience.
A crude, mature parody hack of Red/Blue with raunchy dialogue and humor; intended for adult audiences.
A faithful recreation of the famous 'Pokémon Black' creepypasta, featuring the ghost Pokémon and the screen-blackening 'Curse' move.
The final F-Zero game, a fast futuristic racer with a track editor; released only in Japan and played in English via fan translation.
A FireRed hack set in the new Soala region with an original story (left unfinished after the 4th gym).

Proper Name: Dragon Ball Z: Team Training (FireRed hack)
Description: A FireRed hack reskinned as Dragon Ball Z (DBZ: Team Training) where you catch and train DBZ characters instead of Pokémon, with 130+ new attacks.
A FireRed hack that gives many Pokémon brand-new alternate evolutions, changing how familiar species develop.
A FireRed hack that retells the anime's Kanto journey, letting you play as Ash and recreate iconic episodes.
A large-scale Emerald hack by Reshiram with three leagues, 18 gyms, 600+ Pokémon, the Fairy type, and multiple regions.
An Emerald hack that merges Pokémon with League of Legends, letting you collect animated Champions and battle through Valoran.
A story-heavy FireRed hack by Baro (creator of Quartz), set in the country of Lowheaven with original Pokémon and dark themes of mortality.

Proper Name: Pokémon Ash Kanto: Mewtwo Strikes Back (FireRed hack)
Description: A FireRed hack (Ash Kanto: Mewtwo Strikes Back) recreating the events of the first Pokémon movie with you playing as Ash.

Proper Name: Pokémon Mirage of Tales (Emerald hack by Avara)
Description: An Emerald hack with choice-based storytelling and a class/career system (Ranger, Criminal, Journalist, etc.) on a modern battle engine.
A FireRed hack, Sovereign of the Skies, featuring an original region and a story centered on legendary sky Pokémon.
A FireRed hack by Ephraim225 set in a 'Candyland' Kanto where everything is made of sweets; you catch 151 dessert-themed Pokésweets, use a 12-flavor ice-cream type chart, and cook recipes.
A FireRed hack that faithfully recreates Pokémon Yellow with light balance tweaks and quality-of-life improvements.
A fan-made strategy-RPG campaign built on Fire Emblem: The Sacred Stones (FE8), with an original story and maps.
An acclaimed full-length Fire Emblem fan campaign (FE7) — one of the first 'complete' hacks — following a rebellion against an empire.
A story-driven Fire Emblem fan campaign (FE7) of ~26 chapters about feuding noble houses.

A short, polished mini-hack by Cutlerine (made for a PokéCommunity Speedhacking contest) where Team Rocket is blowing up Route 1 to catch a Cubone, with sharp, funny dialogue and the option to dress as a Rocket grunt.

A LeafGreen hack by dbzmay set in the new Kuland region, where a bullied, poor kid is tasked with toppling a usurper and his eight genetically-modified 'tyrant' Pokémon; Pokémon up to Gen 5 and a god-battling post-game.
An Emerald hack from the Vega team, paired with Sirius, set in an original region full of new Fakemon (Japanese, fan-translated).

A FireRed hack by ice_nine that recreates the first 82 episodes of the anime — the St. Anne, Sabrina's gym, the banned episodes, singing Jigglypuff — with no random trainers or wild Pokémon, just Ash's scripted journey.
A FireRed hack where badges come from quests and tasks rather than gyms, with a story about a mysterious black orb and Team Zen.
A Ruby hack by Gerokunz adding new characters, events, and Johto/Kanto Pokémon while keeping the core Hoenn story.
A mature, realistic Emerald hack set in the gritty 'Outlaw' universe where you play as a police officer.
An infamous bootleg FireRed hack with Fakemon that was sold as a legitimate retail game in stores.
A FireRed hack made by 4chan's /vp/ board, packed with crude humor, memes, and 386 original Fakemon.

A quality-of-life Emerald hack by SkyVortex that keeps the original story but fixes the PRNG glitch, adds more catchable legendaries and day/night events, unlimited TMs, run indoors, and a mild difficulty bump for veterans.
A Ruby-based remake of Gen 2 (Gold/Silver/Crystal), bringing Johto into the Hoenn engine with updated graphics.

Proper Name: Pokémon Dark Cry: The Legend of Giratina
Description: A dark-themed FireRed hack (Dark Cry: The Legend of Giratina) with a Shadow Plate ritual story, new region, and Fakemon.
A notoriously difficult FireRed hack with an original region, Fakemon, and a save-the-world plot.
The sequel to Dark Rising, continuing the hard-as-nails FireRed-based saga.
An entry in the Dark Rising series, continuing its challenging original storyline.
A FireRed hack with a frosty winter theme — snowy towns, icy tunnels, and a quest to uncover who 'Frigo' is.
A FireRed hack by Sloo set in Kanto where you're pulled into Team Rocket by your brother and rise through its ranks to eventually lead it; a completed ~30-hour adventure with all Gen 1-3 Pokémon.
A FireRed hack where you play as rival Gary Oak, reliving his untold heroics across Kanto.
A Ruby hack set in the Lunoh region with 11 gyms, 65 Fakemon, and a plot to summon Giratina.
An Emerald hack with a Johto-inspired original region and modern features like the Physical/Special split and Fairy type.
A Ruby hack blending Pokémon with Harvest Moon — seasons, farming, and a love/relationship mechanic, with Show Owners replacing gyms.
A challenging FireRed hack set in the forgotten Ontak region, a ~25-hour adventure with a darker tone.
A dark Emerald hack billed as one of the most mature hacks, with a crime story about a deadly Pokémon drug and police investigation.
A FireRed hack set years after Gold's victory, spanning Kanto, Johto, and new regions with sidequests and minigames.

A Ruby hack by Corvus of the Black Night that parodies the Golden Sun games (you play as Ivan, with Golden Sun characters as gym leaders), packed with Fakemon, a telepathy mechanic, and wild cameos like the Mythbusters and Daleks.

A dark, violent FireRed revenge hack (the name means 'to kill') inspired by Kill Bill: a kidnapped girl escapes slavery and hunts the criminals who destroyed her family, across the lawless Sevii Islands and a grim post-Outlaw Kanto.
A FireRed hack (LeafYellow) adding new features and content on the Kanto framework.
A FireRed hack set in the Chinese-mythology-inspired Fenju region, with a revenge story and village-elder battles instead of gyms.
A FireRed hack that recreates Pokémon Yellow, with Pikachu following the player.
A FireRed hack by Christos set in the Soala region; a Hack of the Year 2006 winner (left unfinished past the first gym).
An Emerald hack about creating powerful Pokémon, with an original region and an evil-scientist storyline.
A FireRed hack where nearly every wild encounter is a Normal-type, themed around proving Normal types aren't weak.
A FireRed hack recreating the anime's Orange Islands arc.
A FireRed hack with an original fantasy region and entirely new Fakemon, built over eight years of development.
A gritty, mature FireRed hack where you play a homeless street kid in a crime-ridden city.
A FireRed hack by EViLGRiN with a psychic/mind-bending theme and original story.
A famously bizarre Ruby hack with surreal fan-made Fakemon and an odd, much-memed translation.
An Emerald hack notable for adding multiplayer co-op, letting friends explore together.
A FireRed hack based on the Pokémon Adventures manga (the Red/Adventure Chapter).

Proper Name: Pokémon Ruby Destiny: Rescue Rangers (Ruby hack)
Description: A Ruby hack styled after Pokémon Mystery Dungeon, where you become a Pikachu and join a rescue team.
An Emerald hack by Wind1158 where you play Logan/Alma battling the resurgent Team Mirage across three regions (Sylon, Tyron, Johto), with Pokémon up to Gen 7 and Mega Evolution.
A FireRed hack where you play a Team Rocket researcher running missions and experiments for Giovanni.
A FireRed hack telling a Team Rocket-side story across a reimagined Kanto with 380+ Pokémon.

Proper Name: Pokémon Ruby Destiny: Reign of Legends
Description: The first Ruby Destiny game by destinedjagold, set in the Gento region with a multiple-realities story, Gen 4 Pokémon, custom Pokémon, and Shadow Lugia.

Proper Name: Pokémon Ruby Destiny: Life of Guardians
Description: The third Ruby Destiny game by destinedjagold, set 10 years later in the Hevah region: a girl chasing her dream of becoming a Guardian Trainer is swept into a fight against the Dark Organization and a world-threatening shadow.
A FireRed remake of Gold/Silver (Johto) by Zel, an updated 'Sigma' version with extra features.
A FireRed hack set in the Voultan region; popular but never fully completed.
A FireRed hack by Pokepal17 with a time-travel story and a new region, themed around Suicune, Entei, and Raikou.

A darker reimagining of Hoenn (by DarkDon43) where you play Brendan caught between two warring criminal teams, Ander and Cypher, unraveling secrets around a mysterious figure named Yiki; adds Gen 4-5 Pokémon and a choose-your-order Elite Four.
A FireRed hack that recreates Pokémon Yellow on the GBA engine.
A FireRed hack by megiddo set across the Orre and X regions, with a spy-style story fighting a mafia organization.
A Ruby hack by Bazza where you play a Team Rocket recruit stealing badges across the Topaz region; the entire Hoenn dex is replaced with 200+ original Fakemon.
A Touhoumon hack (on FireRed) where Pokémon are replaced by Touhou-themed 'puppets', set in a new world.
The core Touhoumon (Touhou Puppet Play) hack, replacing every Pokémon with a character from the Touhou shooter series.
A FireRed hack with an all-new region and 180+ original Fakemon; originally Japanese, fan-translated to English.
A story-driven Emerald hack set in the Tyron region with an original plot.
An Emerald hack that brings Kalos, Gen 6 Pokémon, and Mega Evolution to the GBA.
A multicart combining the GBC Pokémon Trading Card Game with its fan-translated Japan-only sequel — two card-battling RPGs on one cart.
An Emerald enhancement hack that expands Hoenn with extra Pokémon, features, and a refreshed experience.
A FireRed hack by cooley where you play as Silver, with a new region and mission-based story.
A hugely popular Ruby hack set in the Zhery and Lauren regions, with two leagues and a large adventure.
A FireRed remake of Pokémon Gold (Johto) by Zel — one of the classic remake hacks.
The GBA/FireRed version of the 'Pokémon Black' creepypasta recreation, with the ghost-and-Curse horror premise.

Proper Name: Pokémon Dark Cry: The Legend of Giratina
Description: A dark-story FireRed hack (Dark Cry: The Legend of Giratina) involving a Shadow Plate, a new region, and increased difficulty.
A modern, extremely difficult FireRed hack with current-gen mechanics, the full National Dex, and a punishing Hardcore mode.
A FireRed creepypasta/horror hack centered on a deadly 'Curse' move and a dark, scary atmosphere.
A roguelike Emerald hack with randomized, run-based progression and a tough, replayable structure.
A very popular Emerald hack set in the Tunod region, with cross-generation Pokémon and a sprawling story.
A Ruby hack (Ruby: Snakewood) that turns Hoenn into a zombie apocalypse, full of dark and absurd humor.
An acclaimed FireRed hack set in the Orbtus region, with an ancient-civilization story, polished maps, and modern features.
An Emerald 'Moemon' hack that replaces Pokémon sprites with anime-girl personifications.

Proper Name: Fire Emblem: The Binding Blade (fan translation)
Description: Fire Emblem: The Binding Blade (FE6), Roy's Japan-only strategy RPG, played in English via fan translation.
An Emerald hack that rebalances and streamlines Hoenn for a cleaner, modern playthrough.
An Emerald hack with an expanded Hoenn, new areas, and a story around sky and time legendaries.
A FireRed remake of Pokémon Crystal (Johto), with added islands and extra content.
A FireRed 'Moemon' hack replacing Pokémon with cute anime-girl versions.
A crude comedy FireRed hack with outrageous humor and an offbeat story about a brash playboy trainer.
A FireRed hack that improves the base game, making all 386 Pokémon obtainable in one cartridge.
An infamously brutal difficulty hack of Emerald where gym leaders run full competitive teams; a definitive GBA challenge run.
A GBA compilation of the first two Mother games (EarthBound Beginnings + EarthBound), quirky turn-based RPGs; Japan-only, fan-translated.
A FireRed hack (Rocket Edition by Colonelsalt) where you play a Team Rocket member and can steal Pokémon from defeated trainers.
An Emerald hack by Nemo622 with a cozy, Game Boy Color-style aesthetic, scuba minigames, and a wishing-well feature.
A top-tier FireRed hack set in the Borrius region, praised for its rich story, side content, and modern features.
An Emerald hack where Mario characters become the catchable 'Mariomon', set in a Mushroom Kingdom region (sponsored by Alpharad).
The acclaimed Japan-only sequel to EarthBound, an emotional turn-based RPG, played in English via a renowned fan translation.

Proper Name: Yū Yū Hakusho: Makyō Tōitsusen (fan translation)
Description: A four-player anime fighting game by Treasure based on Yu Yu Hakusho; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.
A hack of Mortal Kombat II that unlocks/restores hidden characters and tweaks the fighting game.
A Japan-only horizontal-scrolling shoot-'em-up; played in English via fan translation.
A festive side-scrolling platformer released in PAL/Australian territories (never in the US).
An unreleased Genesis port of Dragon's Lair: The Adventure Continues, a platformer; survives as a leaked prototype.
A cancelled platformer prototype based on the 7-Up mascot Fido Dido.
A Japan-only entry in the Golden Axe beat-'em-up series (released in the US only via Sega Channel).
An unreleased Simpsons spin-off platformer (The Itchy & Scratchy Game) that survives only as a prototype.
A cancelled top-down action game based on the B-movie cult license; near-complete prototype, scrapped over a fatal bug.
A Japan-only action-RPG by Sega; played in English via fan translation.
A Japan-only tactical strategy RPG; played in English via fan translation.

Proper Name: Brave Battle Saga: The Legend of the Magic Warrior (fan translation)
Description: Brave Battle Saga: The Legend of the Magic Warrior, a Taiwanese Final Fantasy-style RPG; fan-translated to English.
Monster World IV, a Japan-only action-platformer RPG (later officially localized); played here via fan translation.

Proper Name: Fushigi no Umi no Nadia (fan translation)
Description: A Japan-only game based on the anime Nadia: The Secret of Blue Water; played in English via fan translation.
A Japan-only behind-the-back rail shooter in the cute 'Cotton' series; fan-translated.
A Japan-only console port of Sega's classic arcade maze game Pengo.
A Game Freak action-platformer with an electric hero; Japan-only (US via Sega Channel), played in English via fan translation.
An unreleased puzzle/rescue game that survives only as a prototype.
Slap Fight MD, a Japan-only console port of the vertical arcade shoot-'em-up.
Snow Bros.: Nick & Tom, a Japan-only port of the single-screen arcade platformer.
A Sonic 2 hack (Sonic Classic Heroes) that combines Sonic 1 and 2 and lets you play as a team of Sonic, Tails, and Knuckles.
An unreleased beat-'em-up based on the Stone Protectors cartoon/toy line; completed but never released, leaked as a prototype.
The famously unreleased Sega Mega Drive version of Tetris, pulled before launch over licensing; an extremely rare prototype.
An intense Treasure run-and-gun with relentless boss battles; released in PAL/Japan (US only via Sega Channel).
An unreleased futuristic sports game (a port of Brutal Sports Football); survives as a near-complete prototype.
A Japan-only vertical-scrolling shoot-'em-up by Data East; fan-translated.

Proper Name: Lobo (cancelled DC Comics fighting game; prototype)
Description: A cancelled DC Comics fighting game with pre-rendered sprites (à la Killer Instinct); survives as a prototype.
A colorful side-scrolling platformer starring a squirrel hero; a PAL release.

Proper Name: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers (1994 fighting game)
Description: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers (1994), a one-on-one competitive fighting game in the Street Fighter mold.
A Japan-only top-down run-and-gun shooter with a magical theme; fan-translated.
A Japan-only fantasy-themed shooter/action game; fan-translated.

Proper Name: Bare Knuckle III (Japanese Streets of Rage 3)
Description: The Japanese version of Streets of Rage 3, a beat-'em-up that differs from the censored Western release in story and difficulty.
A 32X 3D space-combat shooter; a PAL release showcasing the add-on's polygon graphics.
Advanced Busterhawk: Gleylancer, a Japan-only horizontal shoot-'em-up; fan-translated.
An unreleased platformer by Probe/Ocean; fully playable but cancelled, surviving as a prototype.
Mega Man: The Wily Wars, 16-bit remakes of Mega Man 1-3 plus a new Wily Tower mode (a non-US cartridge release).

Proper Name: The S Factor: Sonia and Silver (Sonic 1 hack)
Description: The S Factor: Sonia and Silver, a Sonic 1 hack adding two new playable characters with unique moves.

Proper Name: Super Mario Bros. (Genesis homebrew port by Mairtrus)
Description: A homebrew port of the original NES Super Mario Bros. to the Genesis (by Mairtrus).

Proper Name: Sonic the Hedgehog: The Lost Worlds (Sonic 2 hack)
Description: Sonic the Hedgehog: The Lost Worlds, a Sonic 2 hack that recreates the zones cut from the game during development.
An ambitious Sonic 1 hack with new playable characters, remixed levels, and extra abilities.

Proper Name: Battle Mania Daiginjō (Trouble Shooter Vintage) (fan translation)
Description: Battle Mania Daiginjo (Trouble Shooter Vintage), a Japan-only run-and-gun shooter; fan-translated.
A rare, highly regarded Japan-only horizontal shoot-'em-up; fan-translated.
A hack of Super Smash Bros. (N64) adding extra characters, stages, music, and rebalanced fighting-game play.

Proper Name: Sin and Punishment: Successor of the Earth (fan translation)
Description: A Treasure on-rails action shooter; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Dawn & Dusk
Description: An Ocarina of Time hack with new dungeons and a reworked overworld.

Proper Name: Mario: Escape from the Jail (Super Mario 64 hack)
Description: A Super Mario 64 hack (a Ztar Attack prequel) where Mario must break out of an abandoned jail across new courses.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: The Missing Link
Description: An Ocarina of Time hack offering a full original adventure set between OoT and Majora's Mask.
A short, meme-famous Super Mario 64 hack built around Waluigi and a taco stand.
A Super Mario 64 hack where you play Luigi investigating disappearances in New Block City, with clue-gathering gameplay.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask – Master Quest (hack)
Description: A harder, remixed version of Majora's Mask with modified dungeons and increased difficulty.
A Mario Kart 64 reskin (Dragon Ball Kart 64) replacing racers with Dragon Ball characters and voice clips.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: The Master of Time
Description: An Ocarina of Time total-conversion hack with an all-new story and world.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time - Nimpize Adventure
Description: An acclaimed Ocarina of Time total conversion (Nimpize Adventure) with a new overworld, dungeons, and quest.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time Master Quest
Type: Official (Master Quest; originally unreleased on N64)
Description: Nintendo's remixed-dungeon version of Ocarina of Time (originally the unreleased N64 'Ura Zelda', later an official GameCube disc).
A Super Mario 64 hack adding new custom courses and stars to collect.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Voyager of Time (OoT hack)
Description: An Ocarina of Time hack with an original, darker story set after Majora's Mask, plus new dungeons.

Proper Name: Adventure Island IV (Takahashi Meijin no Bōken Jima IV) (fan translation)
Description: A Japan-only entry in the Adventure Island platformer series; played in English via fan translation.
Castlevania: Chorus of Mysteries, a full original fan campaign built on the first NES Castlevania.
EarthBound Beginnings (Mother), the NES original that started the series; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation (or the unreleased English prototype).
The Japan-only NES sequel Final Fantasy II, a turn-based RPG; played in English via fan translation.
A Halloween-themed hack of Kirby's Adventure with spooky enemy sprites and remixed levels.

Proper Name: My Little Pony: Dr. Discord's Conquest (Mega Man 3 hack)
Description: My Little Pony: Dr. Discord's Conquest, a My Little Pony-themed hack of Mega Man 3.
Summer Carnival '92: Recca, an intense, bullet-heavy Japan-only vertical shoot-'em-up.
A graphics hack of the NES game Alien Syndrome, redrawing the characters as Rick and Morty.
Splatterhouse: Wanpaku Graffiti, a cute parody spin-off of the horror series; Japan-only, fan-translated.
A Japan-only survival-horror RPG and key precursor to Resident Evil; played in English via fan translation.
A hack of the NES game Ninja Gaiden, reskinned and expanded into a Deadpool action-platformer.

Proper Name: Bishōjo Senshi Sailor Moon: Another Story (fan translation)
Description: Bishoujo Senshi Sailor Moon: Another Story, a Japan-only turn-based RPG; played in English via fan translation.
An acclaimed action-RPG by Quintet about reviving the world; released in English in PAL regions but never in the US.

Proper Name: BS The Legend of Zelda (Satellaview, Map 1 & 2 editions)
Description: BS The Legend of Zelda, Satellaview broadcast Zelda remakes (Map 1 & 2 editions); Japan-only, played via fan translation.

Proper Name: Fire Emblem: Genealogy of the Holy War (Seisen no Keifu) (fan translation)
Description: Fire Emblem: Genealogy of the Holy War, a Japan-only tactical RPG with a multi-generational story; fan-translated.
A brutally difficult Super Mario World 'kaizo' hack demanding frame-perfect platforming.
A Square strategy RPG with dragon-raising mechanics; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.
Mega Man & Bass (Rockman & Forte), a Japan-only SNES action-platformer; played in English via fan translation.
An ambitious unofficial fan sequel to Chrono Trigger (Crimson Echoes), built as a ROM hack of the original.

Proper Name: Fire Emblem: Mystery of the Emblem (Monshō no Nazo) (fan translation)
Description: Fire Emblem: Mystery of the Emblem, a Japan-only tactical RPG; played in English via fan translation.
Fire Emblem: Thracia 776, a deep Japan-only tactical RPG; played in English via fan translation.

Proper Name: Bishōjo Senshi Sailor Moon R (Super Famicom, fan translation)
Description: Bishoujo Senshi Sailor Moon R, a Japan-only side-scrolling beat-'em-up with a versus mode; fan-translated.

Proper Name: Seiken Densetsu 3 / Trials of Mana (fan translation)
Description: Seiken Densetsu 3 (later officially released as Trials of Mana), an action-RPG; Japan-only on SNES, fan-translated.
Shin Megami Tensei if..., a school-set spin-off of the SMT RPG series; Japan-only, fan-translated.
Shin Megami Tensei, the first-person dungeon RPG that anchors the series; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.
Shin Megami Tensei II, the sequel RPG; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.
A tri-Ace action-RPG with real-time battles and branching paths; Japan-only on SNES, fan-translated.
A difficulty/exploration hack of Super Metroid with a redesigned, tougher Zebes.
The first Tales action-RPG, technically impressive for the SNES; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.

Proper Name: Ys IV: Mask of the Sun (fan translation)
Description: Ys IV: Mask of the Sun, an action-RPG in the Ys series; Japan-only, fan-translated.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Parallel Worlds
Hack Of: Zelda: A Link to the Past
Description: A famously hard total-conversion hack of A Link to the Past with new dungeons and overworld.

Proper Name: Dragon Quest V: Hand of the Heavenly Bride (fan translation)
Description: Dragon Quest V: Hand of the Heavenly Bride, a turn-based RPG with monster recruiting; SNES version fan-translated.
A My Little Pony-themed hack of EarthBound, reworking the RPG with pony characters.
A Satellaview broadcast F-Zero racer (BS F-Zero Grand Prix 2); Japan-only, played via fan translation.
An action-RPG based on the Magic Knight Rayearth anime/manga; Japan-only, fan-translated.

Proper Name: Super Back to the Future Part II (fan translation)
Description: Super Back to the Future Part II, a Japan-only side-scrolling action-platformer; fan-translated.

Proper Name: The Violinist of Hamelin (Hameln no Violin Hiki) (fan translation)
Description: A platformer based on the Violinist of Hamelin manga; Japan-only, played in English via fan translation.

Proper Name: Dragon Quest VI: Realms of Revelation (fan translation)
Description: Dragon Quest VI: Realms of Revelation, a turn-based RPG with a dual-world story; SNES version fan-translated.
A short but acclaimed, story-heavy EarthBound hack (the 'Halloween Hack'/Brandish) with a dark, mature tone.
BS Excitebike: Bunbun Mario Battle, a Satellaview racing game with Mario characters; Japan-only, fan-translated.

Proper Name: JoJo's Bizarre Adventure (SNES RPG, fan translation)
Description: A Japan-only SNES RPG based on JoJo's Bizarre Adventure: Stardust Crusaders, mixing adventure and RPG combat; fan-translated.
Parodius Da!, Konami's comedic parody of its Gradius shoot-'em-ups; Japan-only on SNES, fan-translated.

Proper Name: Shodai Nekketsu Kōha Kunio-kun (aka River City Ransom 2; fan translation)
Description: Shodai Nekketsu Kouha Kunio-kun (called 'River City Ransom 2' by fans), a Kunio beat-'em-up RPG; Japan-only, fan-translated.

Proper Name: Shin Megami Tensei (original / older fan translation patch)
Description: Shin Megami Tensei (the original RPG) with an earlier/older fan-translation patch applied.
A difficulty hack of Super Metroid with tougher enemies and a tweaked layout.

Proper Name: The Legend of Zelda: Goddess of Wisdom
Hack Of: Zelda: A Link to the Past
Description: A full total-conversion hack of A Link to the Past with an all-new quest, dungeons, and map.
A Japan-only point-and-click survival-horror game about escaping a killer; played in English via fan translation.

Hack Of: Zelda: A Link to the Past
Description: A Link to the Past hack starring Conker the squirrel in a comedic reskin.
An add-on hack of Zombies Ate My Neighbors providing a fresh set of levels for the co-op horror shooter.
Final Fantasy V, a job-system turn-based RPG; SNES version was Japan-only and is played in English via fan translation.
An exploration/redesign hack of Super Metroid that reworks the map and progression.
Live A Live, an inventive RPG of seven separate scenarios across different eras; Japan-only on SNES (later remade), fan-translated.
